title: Sequential Covariate Shift Detection Using Classifier Two-Sample Tests

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## SEQUENTIAL COVARIATE SHIFT DETECTION USING CLASSIFIER TWO-SAMPLE TESTS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

A standard assumption in supervised learning is that the training data and test data are from the same distribution. However, this assumption often fails to hold in practice, which can cause the learned model to perform poorly. We consider the problem of detecting _covariate shift_ , where the covariate distribution shifts but the conditional distribution of labels given covariates remains the same. This problem can naturally be solved using a two-sample test— _i.e._ , test whether the current test distribution of covariates equals the training distribution of covariates. Our algorithm builds on _classifier tests_ , which train a discriminator to distinguish train and test covariates, and then use the accuracy of this discriminator as a test statistic. A key challenge is that classifier tests assume given a fixed set of test covariates. In practice, test covariates often arrive sequentially over time—e.g., a self-driving car observes a stream of images while driving. Furthermore, covariate shift can occur multiple times— _i.e._ , shift and then shift back later or gradually shift over time. To address these challenges, our algorithm trains the discriminator online. Furthermore, it evaluates test accuracy using each new covariate before taking a gradient step; this strategy avoids constructing a held-out test set, which can reduce sample efficiency. We prove that this optimization preserves the correctness— _i.e._ , our algorithm achieves a desired bound on the false positive rate. In our experiments, we show that our algorithm efficiently detects covariate shifts on ImageNet.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

A key challenge facing deep neural networks is their sensitivity to changes in the data distribution. In particular, supervised learning traditionally assumes that the training and test data are from the same distribution (Vapnik, 1998), but this assumption often fails in practice. For example, an autonomous car using perception to identify obstacles needs to be robust to shifts such as changes in the weather and lighting conditions. We focus on _covariate shift_ (Shimodaira, 2000), where there is a shift in the covariate distribution _p_ ( _x_ ), and the conditional label distribution _p_ ( _y | x_ ) remains unchanged. Covariate shift can reduce model performance (Sugiyama & M¨uller, 2005), invalidate uncertainty estimates (Ovadia et al., 2019; Park et al., 2020), and affect model selection (Sugiyama et al., 2007).

One strategy is to devise an algorithm to detect covariate shift; if detected, the algorithm can alert the user that predictions may be unreliable. Covariate shift detection can be formulated as two-sample hypothesis test (Gretton et al., 2012a; Rabanser et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2020), where the goal is to determine whether two sets of examples are from the same distribution. To test for covariate shift, we choose the first sample to be the data used to train the model and the second sample to be recent test data given as input to the model. Then, the detector returns “covariate shift” if the hypothesis test indicates that the two samples are from different distributions and “no shift” otherwise.

We propose a detection algorithm based on classifier tests (Lopez-Paz & Oquab, 2017; Cheng & Cloninger, 2019; Kim et al., 2021), which use the accuracy of a classifier trained to distinguish the two samples as the test statistic. In particular, if the two samples are from the same distribution, then the accuracy should be 1 _/_ 2; otherwise, it should be _>_ 1 _/_ 2. Since the test statistic follows a binomial distribution, we use the Clopper-Pearson interval (Clopper & Pearson, 1934) (an exact confidence interval for the unknown success probability of the Binomial distribution) to derive the cutoff. In contrast, prior work relies on asymptotics to derive the cutoff, which results in approximations.

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

A key challenge is that the test examples are typically obtained over time—e.g., an autonomous robot continuously perceives its environment, and we want to detect if its distribution of observations shifts at any time. There are two key challenges to leveraging classifier tests in this setting. First, they rely on training a classifier to distinguish training and test examples; doing so on every step would be computationally intractable. Second, they rely on a held-out test set to estimate the test statistic, but constructing such a set online would reduce sample efficiency.

Rather than train a classifier at each step, our proposed algorithm trains a model online using stochastic gradient descent. Then, rather than construct a held-out test set, our algorithm evaluates the accuracy of the model online using each example before taking a gradient step on that example. We prove that this strategy results in an unbiased estimate of the model accuracy; as a consequence, the finite-sample guarantees on the false positive rate provided by the sequential test continue to hold. In addition, we prove bounds on the false negative rate under mild conditions on the classifier (i.e., it achieves nontrivial accuracy distinguishing the two distributions).

We evaluate our approach on both synthetic and natural shifts on the ImageNet (Russakovsky et al., 2015) dataset. In particular, we demonstrate that our approach achieves better sample efficiency than baseline algorithms; furthermore, it uniformly satisfies the desired false positive rate. Thus, our algorithm is an effective strategy for sequential covariate shift detection.

**Contributions.** We formulate (sequential) covariate shift detection as a two-sample test, and propose a novel algorithm to solve this problem (Section 3). Then, we prove finite sample bounds on false positive rate and false negative rate achieved by our algorithm (Section 4). Finally, we empirically demonstrate that our algorithm effectively detects shifts on ImageNet (Section 5).

**Sequential detection vs. sequential tests.** While we consider the sequential setting, we deliberately choose not use a sequential hypothesis test, since the covariate shift may occur after a delay or gradually over time. A sequential test only applies if _all_ of the test data is shifted. Furthermore, since we are not using sequential tests, the false positive rate bound only holds per-step, not uniformly across all steps. This is necessary: we cannot guarantee that we detect a covariate shift occurring at a later point in time if we constrain the false positive to be bounded uniformly across all steps. In our experiments, we show that the rate of false alarms remains manageable while enabling our algorithm to detect covariate shift in a number of interesting scenarios.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Covariate shift.** There has been work on training models in the presence of covariate shift. In particular, in the unsupervised domain adaptation setting (Ben-David et al., 2007; Bickel et al., 2007; Ganin et al., 2016), the algorithm has access to labeled examples from the source domain but only unlabeled examples from the target domain, and the goal is to train a model that achieves good performance on the target domain. One strategy is to use importance weighting to upweight source examples that are more similar to target examples (Bickel et al., 2007). Another strategy is to first learn an invariant representation (Ganin et al., 2016), which is an embedding space where the source and target examples are similar, and then train a model on this embedding space using the source examples. If we detect covariate shift, one solution is to retrain the model using these techniques.

**Two-sample tests.** We focus on _classifier two-sample tests (C2ST)_ . In this approach, the idea is to train a binary classifier to distinguish source and target samples, compute a real-valued score based on this classifier as the test statistic, and then use a univariate two-sample test to determine the cutoff for rejecting the null hypothesis (Friedman, 2004). A natural test statistic is the classifier’s accuracy on a held-out test set (Kim et al., 2021; Lopez-Paz & Oquab, 2017), or the differences in the classifier’s logits (Cheng & Cloninger, 2019); in this work, we use the former. One way to compute the cutoff is to use the asymptotic distribution of the test statistic (Lopez-Paz & Oquab, 2017). Nonparametric tests such as permutation tests can also be used (Kim et al., 2021).

Another kind of two-sample test is called a _kernel two-sample test_ . In this approach, the idea is to use the maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) between the two samples according to a given kernel embedding as the test statistic (Gretton et al., 2012a; Chwialkowski et al., 2015; Jitkrittum et al., 2016). The key design decision is the choice of kernel. One strategy is to use a nonparametric kernel such as Gaussian radial basis functions (Gretton et al., 2012a); alternatively, the kernel can also be optimized to minimize the false negative rate of the resulting test (Gretton et al., 2012b). In addition,

recent work has shown how to first learn a kernel function in the form of a deep neural network, and then evaluate the MMD distance on a held-out test set (Liu et al., 2020). The test statistic can be chosen based on finite sample bounds or based on its asymptotic distribution (Gretton et al., 2012a). Nonparametric permutation tests can also be used (Liu et al., 2020) with this approach.

**Concept Drift.** In the context of _concept drift_ (Gama et al., 2014), there has been work detecting shifts in _p_ ( _x, y_ ) (Gonc¸alves Jr et al., 2014). However, this work assumes that ground truth labels are provided for test examples, whereas our approach only requires unlabeled test examples. The former is substantially easier, since it suffices to check for drift in the distribution of prediction errors, which is usually very simple (e.g., a Bernoulli distribution for the 0-1 loss), making it easy to test for drift. In contrast, our approach checks for drift in high-dimensional covariates distribution.

**Sequential hypothesis testing.** A closely related problem is sequential hypothesis testing, which adaptively decides whether to reject the null hypothesis as samples become available (Wald, 1945). These approaches can also applied to two-sample testing (Balsubramani & Ramdas, 2015; Lh´eritier & Cazals, 2018; 2019; Manole & Ramdas, 2021). However, as discussed above, they assume that the each distribution of the two samples does not change over time. In contrast, we are interested in the setting where the test examples might initially be from the same distribution as the training examples, but then shift at a later point in time. Sequential tests are not applicable to this setting.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

We evaluate the effectiveness of our algorithm at detecting both natural and synthetic covariate shifts of varying forms (e.g., gradual shifts and multiple shifts back and forth), showing that it significantly outperforms natural baselines.

## 5.1 EXPERIMENT SETUP

**Baselines.** We compare our algorithm to three baselines: two of them differ in the way they use the samples at each time step, the third uses Wald’s sequential likelihood test (Wald, 1945). For the first two baselines, while our approach uses all samples to construct the CP interval around the accuracy of the source-target classifier ˆ _gt_ as well as to train ˆ _gt_ , the baseline instead constructs a held-out test set using every _H[th]_ sample. Then, only this held-out test set is used to compute the CP interval, and only the remaining samples are used to train ˆ _gt_ . In our experiments, we used values of _H ∈{_ 2 _,_ 5 _}_ , denoted H2, H5, respectively. For Wald’s test, we consider the Bernoulli random variable with a probability _p_ indicating whether the prediction of the source-target classifier is correct for the given

This baseline is essentially the online version of an existing classifier two-sample test (C2ST) (Kim et al., 2021; Lopez-Paz & Oquab, 2017), which splits the (fixed) training dataset into a training set to train _g_ ˆ _t_ and a held-out test set to estimate the accuracy of _g_ ˆ _t_ ; thus, _H_ controls the tradeoff between the number of examples in the training set and held-out test set.

**Source-target classifier.** We use a fully-connected neural network with a single hidden layer (with ˆ 128 hidden units) and with the ReLU activation functions as the source-target classifier _gt_ . We use a binary cross-entropy loss for training in conjunction with an SGD optimizer with a learning rate of 0.01 (for natural shift experiments) and 0.001 (for synthetic shift experiments). Finally, since the inputs are ImageNet images (Russakovsky et al., 2015), we use a 2048-dimensional feature vector generated by first running a pretrained ResNet152 model (He et al., 2016) on the images, and then using these features vectors for the covariates of _Sw,t_ and _Tw,t_ .

**Scenarios.** We run each algorithm to test whether the target distribution in the given window is shifted with three different scenarios: multiple shift (“M-shift”), gradually increasing shift (“GIshift”), and gradually increasing-then-decreasing shift (“GID-shift”). Table 1 describes each scenario. For example, the multiple shift scenario proceeds as follows: (i) it starts with no covariate shift at the beginning; (ii) after observing 25% target samples ( _i.e._ , 250 _[th]_ samples for natural shift experiments and 2500 _[th]_ samples for synthetic shift experiments), covariate shift is applied to all target samples (with probability 1) by adding random perturbations for synthetic shift and by drawing samples from a target distribution for natural shift; (iii) after 50% of target samples, it reverts to no covariate shift; and (iv) finally after observing 75% target samples, the covariate shift is applied to the all target samples. Gradually increasing shift and gradually increasing-then-decreasing shift scenarios start with no covariate shift for the first 20% of target samples; then, covariate shift is applied with some probability 0 _< p <_ 1 by gradually changing _p_ over time.

**Stream data generation.** For each shift ( _i.e._ , natural shift and synthetic shift), we have a source dataset _S_ and target datasets _Tt_ , from which we randomly draw source and target samples for each time step _t_ . In particular, we consider a batch of samples for computational efficiency, where we denote the batch size by _B_ ; we use _B_ = 10 for our experiments. That is, we wait for _B_ samples to be collected from the target distribution before checking for covariate shift and the updating the sourcetarget classifier; then, we begin collecting the next batch. Finally, we evaluate each approach using multiple random repetitions, which we denote by _R_ (the value of _R_ depends on each experiment).

## 5.2 NATURAL SHIFT

**Dataset.** First, we consider a natural shift on ImageNet. To construct such a shift, we consider the subset of dog classes; in particular, 120 of the 1000 of the ImageNet classes are of dogs (Khosla et al., 2011). Then, we randomly select half ( _i.e._ , 60) of these classes to be the source dataset, and the other half to be the target dataset; thus, the number of source and target images is 2997 each (after removing duplicated images). As a consequence, the source and target datasets correspond to different dog breeds, which is a kind of natural distribution shift.

**Results.** Figure 1 and Table 2 show results for the natural shift experiment with _w_ = 10 and _α_ = 1%. Figure 1 illustrates detection rates of the four algorithms with _R_ = 100 repetitions (i.e., the fraction of repetitions that reported “covariate shift” at each step). Table 2a shows the number

**Discussion.** Figure 1 shows the detection rate of each algorithm as each scenario progresses. In multiple shift (Figure 1a) and gradually increasing-then-decreasing shift (Figure 1c), covariate shift disappears after a certain point, and all algorithms correctly detect this change. However, as shown in Table 2a, our approach always requires fewer samples to detect the shift. While H5 does not achieve 80% detection and H2 and Wald reach 80% only for multiple shift change scenario, our approach always detects covariate shift at a rate higher than 80%. Furthermore, for multiple shift, our algorithm requires fewer than half the number of samples compared to H2. In summary, our algorithm is significantly more sample efficient at detecting covariate shift compared to the baselines, most likely since it utilizes all samples for both training the source-target classifier and constructing the CP interval. For FPR, all algorithms always satisfy the FPR bound ( _i.e._ , FPR _≤ α_ ).

## 5.3 SYNTHETIC SHIFT

**Dataset.** Next, we consider a synthetic shift on ImageNet. In particular, we split the original ImageNet validation set into equal sized source and target datasets. To construct the target dataset, we add synthetic perturbations on original images. We (separately) consider five perturbation types from (Hendrycks & Dietterich, 2019)—in particular, Contrast, Defocus Blur, Elastic Transform, Gaussian Blur, and Gaussian Noise, with five different severity levels.

**Results.** The experiment results are shown in Figure 2 and Table 3 for the experiments with the perturbation severity of 2, window size _w_ = 10, and significance level _α_ = 1%. **Discussion.** As can be seen, our approach outperforms the baselines in terms of sample efficiency for the covariate shift detection as was the case of the natural shift. ## 6 CONCLUSION

We have proposed a novel covariate shift detection algorithm, which uses a classifier two-sample test to check whether the current test examples differ in distribution compared to the training examples. Our approach ensures sample efficiency by avoiding the need to split the dataset into a training set and a held-out test set, and instead using all the data to both train the source-target discriminator and to evaluate its accuracy. We prove that even with this optimization, our approach provides finite sample guarantees on the false positive rate at a desired level; we also prove bounds on the false negative rate under a mild conditions on the trained classifier. Finally, we empirically demonstrate that our proposed algorithm is significantly more sample efficient compared to a natural baseline that uses a held-out test set in terms of detecting both natural and synthetic shifts on ImageNet.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a method for sequential covariate shift detection using classifier two-sample tests with online updates and Clopper-Pearson calibration, aiming to improve sample efficiency while maintaining finite-sample false positive control. It makes a reasonable contribution by adapting C2ST to streaming settings with a theoretically justified reuse of data for both training and testing. The empirical results on ImageNet-based shifts show improved detection speed over baselines that split data, which supports the core claim of sample efficiency.

Overview issues:

- Invalid independence assumption undermines Clopper-Pearson guarantee: The theoretical justification for the false positive rate control relies on Lemma 1, which claims that classifier predictions $\hat{y}_i$ are conditionally independent given past predictions. However, this is not true in practice because $\hat{g}_t$ is updated online using all previous data—so predictions are dependent through the shared, adaptively learned model parameters. The CP interval requires independent Bernoulli trials, but here the trials are neither independent nor identically distributed due to continual learning. As a result, the coverage guarantee in Theorem 3 does not hold under the actual algorithm. This breaks the core theoretical claim of finite-sample false positive control. The authors must either prove conditional independence under online learning (which contradicts standard learning dynamics) or adopt a different inference strategy such as sequential testing, held-out calibration, or conformal prediction with exchangeability assumptions.

- Overlooked temporal dependence in sequential data invalidates binomial model: The method models the accuracy estimate $2w\hat{\mu}_{w,t}$ as a Binomial$(2w, \mu^*_{w,t})$ random variable, assuming independent samples within the window. However, in a sequential setting with potential temporal dependence (e.g., autocorrelation in image streams from a moving camera), the i.i.d. assumption on $x_t$ and $x'_t$ is violated. This invalidates the binomial model used for the CP interval and biases the variance estimate, leading to incorrect confidence intervals and potentially inflated false positive rates. The paper does not test or account for temporal dependence in the ImageNet streams. To fix this, the authors should either demonstrate that their features are approximately independent (e.g., via autocorrelation analysis) or incorporate time-series robustness, such as block bootstrapping or HAC estimators, into the uncertainty quantification.

- Classifier reuse introduces lookahead bias and overfitting risk: The algorithm uses each sample to evaluate the classifier before updating it (Step 4 and 6 in Algorithm 1), but then includes that same sample in the training stream that shapes future versions of the classifier. While this improves sample efficiency, it creates a feedback loop where the evaluation metric is influenced by data that will later be used to optimize the very same classifier. This can lead to overfitting and optimistic bias in the accuracy estimate, especially when the classifier learns slowly. The theoretical analysis assumes the evaluation is independent of training, but in reality the classifier's future behavior is shaped by these samples. A proper fix would be to use a held-out validation buffer or a delayed update scheme to ensure separation between evaluation and training data within the online pipeline.

- Insufficient comparison to modern two-sample testing baselines: The baselines are limited to naive held-out C2ST variants and Wald’s test, but the paper omits comparisons to more powerful and relevant methods such as kernel MMD with learned embeddings (e.g., Liu et al., 2020), sequential MMD tests, or E-value based approaches (Shi et al., 2022). These methods are designed for high-dimensional and streaming data and offer better power and calibration. Without such comparisons, the claimed superiority of the proposed method is not well substantiated. The authors should include at least one kernel-based and one sequential nonparametric test as baselines to position their method within the current state of the art.

- Unrealistic assumption of known training distribution access: The algorithm assumes continuous access to fresh samples from the training distribution $\mathcal{S}$ at every time step (via $x_t \sim \mathcal{S}$ in Algorithm 1). In practice, the training data may not be available during deployment due to storage, privacy, or computational constraints. Most real-world systems only have access to a static model and incoming test data. The method should instead operate on a fixed set of training features or a generative model of $\mathcal{S}$. The current setup limits practical applicability and should be addressed by either evaluating under a finite training buffer or proposing a mechanism for replay or distillation of training distribution characteristics.

- Lack of ablation on classifier capacity and feature representation: The experiments use a fixed ResNet152 feature extractor and a simple MLP classifier, but do not explore how detection performance depends on feature quality or classifier complexity. Since C2ST power depends critically on the ability of the classifier to distinguish distributions, results may vary significantly with architecture choice. The paper should include ablation studies showing detection performance with weaker features (e.g., random projections) or smaller classifiers to assess robustness. Additionally, comparing to raw pixel inputs or other backbones would strengthen the validity of the conclusions.

- No ablation on window size and classifier capacity: The paper claims sample efficiency gains from using all data for training and evaluation, but does not show how performance depends on the window size w or the classifier's capacity. Readers need to know whether the method remains effective with smaller w (e.g., w=5) or weaker models, as this affects practical deployment in resource-constrained settings. To validate robustness, the authors should add an ablation study in Section 5.3 showing detection delay and FPR for w ∈ {5,10,20} and classifier sizes with 32, 64, and 128 hidden units. This would clarify the tradeoffs and operational range of the method.

- Missing comparison to kernel MMD baselines: The paper positions itself against held-out C2ST and Wald’s test but omits comparisons to kernel-based two-sample tests, which are standard in high-dimensional settings. In particular, MMD with a learned deep kernel (e.g., Liu et al., 2020) or adaptive bandwidth selection offers strong finite-sample power and is directly applicable to sequential testing. Without this comparison, the claim of superior sample efficiency is incomplete. The authors should include a baseline using deep kernel MMD on the same ResNet features, with test thresholds calibrated via permutation or block bootstrap, and report detection delay and FPR in Table 3.

- No demonstration of detection under delayed shift: The method is motivated by the need to detect shifts that occur after a delay, yet all experiments assume immediate or gradually increasing shifts starting from step zero. A more meaningful test would be a scenario where the distribution is identical to training for the first 50% of samples, then shifts abruptly. The current M-shift scenario includes such a change, but the shift onset is at 25%, not after extended stationarity. The authors should add a new experiment with no shift for the first 500–1000 samples, then a sudden perturbation, and show that the detector triggers promptly while maintaining FPR control during the initial period.

- Unverified assumption of ResNet features being i.i.d.: The method assumes i.i.d. samples within the sliding window, but ImageNet streams generated by a moving camera may exhibit temporal dependence. The use of ResNet features does not guarantee independence. The paper should verify this assumption by computing autocorrelation of feature norms or pairwise distances over time in the synthetic stream. If dependence is present, the binomial model is invalid. At minimum, the authors should include a diagnostic plot in Appendix D showing autocorrelation up to lag 10, or justify independence by subsampling frames at large intervals.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Prove or correct the independence assumption in Lemma 1; if the current proof is invalid, revise the theoretical framework to account for dependent predictions, possibly using martingale-based or conformal methods.

- Address temporal dependence in the data by either demonstrating i.i.d.-like behavior in the feature stream or modifying the test statistic to account for correlation (e.g., via block averaging or robust variance estimation).

- Introduce a mechanism to prevent data reuse bias, such as a delayed update or separate evaluation buffer, and re-evaluate the false positive rate under this corrected scheme.

- Include comparisons to modern two-sample test baselines such as learned-kernel MMD, sequential MMD, or E-value methods to properly contextualize performance gains.

- Evaluate the method under a more realistic setting where only a finite set of training features is available, rather than continuous access to $\mathcal{S}$.

comments.title: Invalid conditional independence assumption undermines CP interval
comments.quote:
###### Lemma 1.

If $x_{i},\ldots,x_{j}$ are independent for any $i,j\in\mathbb{N}$ where $i<j$, $\hat{y}_{i},\ldots,\hat{y}_{j}$ are conditionally independent given $\hat{y}_{i},\ldots,\hat{y}_{j-1}$.
comments.explanation: The claim that predictions $\hat{y}_i, \ldots, \hat{y}_j$ are conditionally independent given past predictions fails because the classifier $\hat{g}_t$ is updated online using all prior data. The model parameters depend on the full history of inputs and labels, so predictions are coupled through shared weights. This induces dependence even if inputs are independent, violating the conditional independence required for the binomial model. The Clopper-Pearson interval assumes independent Bernoulli trials, so its coverage guarantee does not hold under this adaptive learning scheme. The proof in Appendix A.1 would need to establish conditional independence under parameter updates, which contradicts standard learning dynamics.

comments.title: Degenerate term invalidates false negative bound
comments.quote:
If $\mathcal{S}\neq\tilde{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}$ and $w-1-\lfloor\sqrt{w\log(2/\alpha)}\rfloor\geq 0$, then we have

$\mathbb{P}\left[\hat{f}(S_{w,t},T_{w,t};\alpha)=1\right]\geq F\left(2w-\lfloor a(w,\alpha)+1\rfloor;2w,\frac{1}{2}-\epsilon\right)+F\Big{(}\lceil b(w,\alpha)-1\rceil;2w,1\Big{)}.$ (3)
comments.explanation: The second term $F(\lceil b(w,\alpha)-1\rceil; 2w, 1)$ involves a binomial CDF with success probability 1, which is degenerate at $2w$. Thus, $F(k; 2w, 1) = 1$ for all $k \geq 0$, making the entire lower bound at least 1—impossible for a probability. This indicates a typo. Given the context, the intended parameter is likely $\frac{1}{2} + \epsilon$ or another value less than 1. Moreover, the first term uses $\frac{1}{2} - \epsilon$, but under $\mu_{w,t}^* \geq \frac{1}{2} + \epsilon$, the distribution should be stochastically larger, so the CDF should reflect a higher success probability. The current expression is mathematically incoherent.

comments.title: Incorrect sign in false negative analysis
comments.quote: Assume $\hat{g}_{t}$ achieves nontrivial accuracy, *i.e.*, $\mu_{w,t}^{*}\geq 1/2+\epsilon$, where $\epsilon\in(0,1/2]$, is the accuracy at distinguishing $\mathcal{S}$ and $\tilde{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}$.
comments.explanation: The false negative bound in Theorem 5 uses $\frac{1}{2} - \epsilon$ in the binomial CDF, which contradicts the assumption that $\mu_{w,t}^* \geq \frac{1}{2} + \epsilon$. If the classifier has accuracy $\frac{1}{2} + \epsilon$, the number of correct predictions should follow $\text{Binomial}(2w, \frac{1}{2} + \epsilon)$, so the CDF in the lower bound should reflect that the observed accuracy exceeds $\frac{1}{2}$. Using $F(\cdot; 2w, \frac{1}{2} - \epsilon)$ suggests the alternative is below $\frac{1}{2}$, which is inconsistent. This suggests a sign error. The detection power should increase with $\epsilon$, but the current expression decreases, which is counterintuitive.

comments.title: Undefined $\tilde{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}$ in accuracy definition
comments.quote:
Let $\mathcal{Q}_{w,t}$ be a distribution over $\mathcal{X}\times\{0,1\}$, where

$\mathcal{Q}_{w,t}(x,y)\!=\!\frac{1}{2}\cdot\mathcal{S}(x)\cdot\mathbbm{1}(y=0)+\frac{1}{2}\cdot\tilde{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}(x)\cdot\mathbbm{1}(y=1).$

Then, $z=\mathbbm{1}(\hat{g}_{t}(x)=y)$ is a Bernoulli random variable with distribution Bernoulli$(\mu_{w,t}^{*})$, where

$\mu_{w,t}^{*}=\mathbb{P}_{(x,y)\sim\mathcal{Q}_{w,t}}[\hat{g}_{t}(x)=y]$
comments.explanation: The term $\tilde{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}$ is used in the definition of $\mathcal{Q}_{w,t}$ but is never defined. While it is reasonable to assume it denotes the empirical distribution of the last $w$ target samples, this is not stated. Without a formal definition, the mathematical object $\mu_{w,t}^*$ is ambiguous, and the probabilistic model lacks rigor. Readers cannot reproduce the setup or verify the correctness of $\hat{\mu}_{w,t}$ as an estimator without knowing the exact form of $\tilde{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}$. Define $\tilde{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}$ explicitly as the empirical distribution $\frac{1}{w} \sum_{i=t-w+1}^t \delta_{x'_i}$.

comments.title: Notational inconsistency in Theorem 3
comments.quote:
###### Theorem 3 (Bound on false positive rate).

If $\mathcal{S}=\tilde{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}$, then for any $\hat{g}_{t}$, we have

$\mathbb{P}_{(S_{w,t},T_{w,t})\sim\mathcal{S}^{w}\times\mathcal{T}_{t-w+1:t}}\left[\hat{f}(S_{w,t},T_{w,t};\alpha)=0\right]\geq 1-\alpha.$ (2)
comments.explanation: The condition $\mathcal{S} = \tilde{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}$ introduces an undefined symbol $\tilde{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}$. In Lemma 2 and surrounding text, the null hypothesis is stated as $\mathcal{S} = \mathcal{T}_{w,t}$, and the probability measure uses $\mathcal{T}_{t-w+1:t}$, which aligns with $\mathcal{T}_{w,t}$ as the recent test distribution over $w$ samples. There is no definition or explanation of $\tilde{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}$. If it is intended to be identical to $\mathcal{T}_{w,t}$, the notation should be unified. If it differs, this must be explicitly defined. As written, the theorem's condition lacks semantic clarity.

comments.title: Incorrect hypothesis specification for Wald test
comments.quote: The hypothesis test is  $H_0: p = 0.5$  vs.  $H_1: p = 0.5 + \epsilon$ , where  $\epsilon = 0.2$  in our experiments; we restart the test each time it makes a decision.
comments.explanation: The alternative hypothesis $H_1: p = 0.5 + \epsilon$ with fixed $\epsilon = 0.2$ incorrectly specifies a point alternative in a sequential likelihood ratio test. Wald’s SPRT requires two fully specified distributions. However, the true $p$ under shift is unknown and not exactly $0.7$, making this a composite alternative. Using a point alternative when the true effect size may vary leads to miscalibrated stopping boundaries and invalid Type I/II error control. The correct approach is to define $H_1: p = p_1 > 0.5$ for a prespecified minimally detectable effect (e.g., $p_1 = 0.7$), not as a deviation from $H_0$. Furthermore, the paper does not report whether the SPRT boundaries were adjusted for the restart mechanism, which affects overall false positive rate.

comments.title: Ambiguous detection threshold interpretation
comments.quote: Table 3a shows the number of target samples required by each algorithm to detect the first covariate shift in the detection rate of at least  $80\%$ .
comments.explanation: The phrase 'detection rate of at least 80%' is ambiguous: it could mean that detection occurs by the reported sample count in 80% of trials, or that the algorithm achieves 80% detection probability at that point. However, the text does not specify how this threshold is computed—whether it's the median detection time across trials, the earliest time at which detection exceeds 80% in simulations, or another rule. Without this, the numbers in Table 3a lack a clear operational meaning. For reproducibility and interpretability, the paper should define detection rate precisely: e.g., 'the smallest $t$ such that $\mathbb{P}(\text{detected by time } t) \geq 0.8)$'.

comments.title: Misleading claim of FPR bound satisfaction
comments.quote: For FPR, all algorithms always satisfy the FPR bound (i.e.,  $\mathrm{FPR} \leq \alpha$ ).
comments.explanation: The claim that all algorithms satisfy $\mathrm{FPR} \leq \alpha = 1\%$ is based on empirical results, but the theoretical justification for FPR control is invalid due to violated independence assumptions. The Clopper-Pearson interval assumes independent trials, but the adaptive classifier induces dependence. While the empirical FPR is below $\alpha$ in these trials, there is no guarantee it would be in general. The result may hold in this simulation, but the reasoning conflates empirical performance with theoretical guarantee. Rewrite to reflect that empirical FPR was below $\alpha$ in these trials, but do not assert it as a general property without stronger justification.

comments.title: FPR interpretation contradicts stated significance level
comments.explanation: The caption states that values exceeding $\alpha = 1\%$ are bolded, but several entries are exactly 1.00%. A false positive rate of 1.00% is equal to the nominal level, not exceeding it. If the threshold is strict ($>1\%$), then no entries should be bolded; if it's inclusive ($\geq1\%$), then 'Ours' in M-shift at 1000 and 2000 steps should be bolded, but they are not. This inconsistency undermines the claim that the method 'always satisfies the FPR bound'. The table must clarify whether the bound is strict or not, and ensure bolding matches.

comments.title: Misleading claim of universal detection
comments.quote: while H5 does not achieve  $80\%$  detection and H2 and Wald reach  $80\%$  only for multiple shift change scenario, our approach always detects covariate shift at a rate higher than  $80\%$
comments.explanation: The claim that 'our approach always detects... higher than 80%' lacks qualification. Figure 1 shows detection rates rising over time; at early steps, the rate is below 80%. The claim should specify that the method eventually exceeds 80% in all scenarios, not that it does so uniformly at every step. As stated, it suggests instantaneous or guaranteed detection, which is not shown. The word 'always' refers to across scenarios, not across time—but this nuance is lost. A reader may interpret this as 100% detection at every step. Add 'eventually' to clarify: 'our approach eventually achieves detection rates above 80% in all scenarios'.

comments.title: Incorrect pluralization undermines clarity
comments.quote: under a mild conditions on the trained classifier
comments.explanation: The phrase 'under a mild conditions' contains a grammatical error: 'a' is singular while 'conditions' is plural. This should be 'under mild conditions' or 'under a mild condition'. Given that the surrounding context refers to multiple conditions (e.g., classifier inaccuracy bounded away from 1/2, sufficient window size), the correct version is 'under mild conditions'. Remove 'a' from the sentence because it introduces an ungrammatical construction that distracts from the technical meaning.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a sequential covariate shift detection algorithm using classifier two-sample tests with online learning. While the core idea of using all samples for both training and testing is clever, the paper has significant issues with its theoretical claims, experimental evaluation, and framing. The independence assumption crucial for the statistical guarantees appears questionable, the false negative rate bound is overly conservative and impractical, and the experimental evaluation lacks proper baselines and analysis of computational requirements.

The paper addresses an important problem in sequential covariate shift detection and presents an elegant algorithmic insight about using samples for both training and testing. The method shows promising empirical results in terms of sample efficiency. However, the theoretical foundations have serious gaps, particularly regarding the independence assumptions required for valid statistical inference, and the experimental evaluation is incomplete without comparisons to state-of-the-art sequential detection methods.

Overview issues:

- Questionable Independence Assumption in Sequential Setting: The paper's key theoretical claim relies on Lemma 1, which states that predictions are conditionally independent given previous predictions. This lemma is used to justify that the accuracy estimate follows a Binomial distribution, enabling the use of Clopper-Pearson intervals. However, in an online learning setting where the classifier is continuously updated on streaming data, this independence assumption is highly suspect. The classifier parameters at time t depend on all previous samples, creating temporal dependencies that violate the i.i.d. assumption required for Binomial distribution. The proof in Appendix A.1 appears to rely on a technicality that doesn't address the core issue of parameter dependence. This undermines the validity of the false positive rate guarantee in Theorem 3, which is a central contribution of the paper.

- Impractical False Negative Rate Bound: The false negative rate bound in Theorem 5 requires w ≥ 201 for α = 0.01, meaning the algorithm needs at least 201 samples per window to provide any meaningful guarantee. This is extremely restrictive for practical sequential detection scenarios where rapid detection is crucial. Moreover, the bound itself is complex and provides little intuition about the actual power of the test. The condition that the classifier achieves accuracy ≥ 1/2 + ε is also problematic - in high-dimensional settings with limited samples, even the optimal classifier may struggle to achieve this threshold. The paper should provide empirical validation of this bound and discuss its practical limitations, rather than presenting it as a meaningful theoretical guarantee.

- Lack of Proper Sequential Baselines: The experimental evaluation compares primarily against batch-based variants (H2, H5) and Wald's sequential test, but omits comparisons with state-of-the-art sequential change detection methods. The literature context mentions several relevant sequential approaches (e.g., Balsubramani and Ramdas 2015, Lhéritier and Cazals 2018, 2019, Manole and Ramdas 2021) that are not included as baselines. The paper argues that sequential tests are not applicable because they assume all test data is shifted, but this is not entirely accurate - many sequential tests can handle delayed or gradual shifts. Without proper comparison to these methods, it's impossible to assess whether the proposed approach truly advances the state of the art in sequential detection.

- Missing Computational Complexity Analysis: The paper claims computational efficiency but provides no analysis of the time or space complexity of the online learning approach. Training a neural network online at each time step could be computationally expensive, especially for high-dimensional data like images. The experiments use a relatively simple classifier (single hidden layer with 128 units), but scalability to larger models or higher-dimensional data is unexplored. There's no discussion of the trade-offs between computational cost and detection performance, nor any comparison of runtime against the baselines. For a method intended for real-time applications like autonomous driving, this is a critical omission.

- Limited Evaluation of Gradual Shift Detection: While the paper includes gradual shift scenarios (GI-shift and GID-shift), the evaluation is insufficient. The gradual shifts are implemented as simple probability mixtures at discrete time points, which doesn't capture the complexity of real-world gradual drift. More importantly, there's no analysis of how the window size w affects detection of gradual shifts - a smaller window might miss slow drifts, while a larger window delays detection. The paper should include a sensitivity analysis of w and demonstrate performance on more realistic gradual shift scenarios, perhaps using synthetic data with smoothly varying distributions.

- Missing worked example of algorithm mechanics: The paper presents the sequential algorithm theoretically but never walks through a concrete toy example showing how the online updates work. Readers need to see how the classifier parameters evolve and how the Clopper-Pearson interval is computed step-by-step for a simple synthetic distribution shift. A minimal example with 1D Gaussian source and shifted target distributions, using w=3-5, would clarify how the algorithm maintains validity while using each sample for both testing and training. This is essential for building intuition about the core technical contribution.

- No demonstration of non-vacuous detection power: The theoretical bounds on false negative rate are impractical (requiring w≥201), and the experiments only show relative performance against weak baselines. The paper needs a concrete example showing the algorithm can detect a specific, known shift with quantified power. For instance, simulate a shift from N(0,1) to N(μ,1) with μ=0.5, compute the theoretical classifier accuracy, then empirically verify the detection rate matches the bound. Without this, readers cannot assess whether the method actually works in regimes where theory provides no guidance.

- Missing guidance on practical implementation: The paper claims computational efficiency but provides no implementation details for real-world deployment. There's no discussion of: (1) how to choose window size w in practice, (2) computational overhead of online neural network updates, (3) memory requirements for storing predictions in the sliding window, (4) handling of batch processing vs. true streaming. A practitioner wanting to deploy this in an autonomous system needs concrete guidance on these engineering aspects, which are completely absent.

- Underdeveloped comparison to sequential tests: The paper dismisses sequential hypothesis tests as inapplicable but never empirically validates this claim. A minimal experiment comparing against a sequential test like SPRT or the methods of Balsubramani and Ramdas (2015) on the same synthetic gradual shift scenarios would demonstrate the claimed limitation. The paper should show either (a) sequential tests fail under gradual shifts, or (b) quantify the performance gap. Without this, the positioning against sequential methods remains unsubstantiated.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Address the independence assumption issue by either providing a rigorous proof that holds for the online learning setting or modifying the theoretical claims to acknowledge the dependence structure

- Include comparisons with state-of-the-art sequential change detection methods from the literature, not just batch variants

- Provide computational complexity analysis and runtime comparisons to demonstrate practical feasibility

- Conduct a thorough sensitivity analysis of the window size parameter and evaluate on more realistic gradual shift scenarios

- Simplify or provide empirical validation of the false negative rate bound, or remove it if it cannot be made practically useful

comments.title: Contradiction between online training and test set reuse
comments.quote: it evaluates test accuracy using each new covariate before taking a gradient step; this strategy avoids constructing a held-out test set, which can reduce sample efficiency
comments.explanation: The claim that evaluating test accuracy before gradient steps "avoids constructing a held-out test set" is logically inconsistent. If each new covariate is used for both testing (accuracy evaluation) and training (gradient step), then the test set is not held out—it's contaminated by training data. This violates the fundamental principle of classifier two-sample tests, which require independent test samples for valid statistical inference. The algorithm cannot simultaneously claim to avoid held-out test sets while using samples for both testing and training. This undermines the claimed statistical validity of the false positive rate bound.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about binomial distribution
comments.quote: In particular, if the two samples are from the same distribution, then the accuracy should be $1/2$; otherwise, it should be $>1/2$. Since the test statistic follows a binomial distribution, we use the Clopper-Pearson interval *(Clopper and Pearson, 1934)* (an exact confidence interval for the unknown success probability of the Binomial distribution) to derive the cutoff.
comments.explanation: The claim that the test statistic follows a binomial distribution is incorrect in this sequential setting. The binomial distribution assumes independent and identically distributed trials, but in the proposed algorithm, the classifier parameters are updated online using stochastic gradient descent on each example before it's used for testing. This creates dependence between trials - the classifier at time t depends on all previous examples, violating the independence assumption required for a binomial distribution. The paper later relies on Lemma 1 to claim conditional independence, but this lemma is controversial and doesn't resolve the fundamental issue that the classifier's parameters evolve over time, creating temporal dependencies in the predictions. Rewrite "Since the test statistic follows a binomial distribution" as "Since the test statistic approximates a binomial distribution under the assumption of conditional independence between predictions" to accurately reflect the reliance on this questionable assumption.

comments.title: Contradiction about false positive rate guarantees
comments.quote: We prove that this strategy results in an unbiased estimate of the model accuracy; as a consequence, the finite-sample guarantees on the false positive rate provided by the sequential test continue to hold. In addition, we prove bounds on the false negative rate under mild conditions on the classifier (i.e., it achieves nontrivial accuracy distinguishing the two distributions).
comments.explanation: This statement contradicts the later clarification that "the false positive rate bound only holds per-step, not uniformly across all steps." The initial claim suggests that finite-sample guarantees continue to hold, implying uniform control, but the later text explicitly states this is not the case. The unbiased accuracy estimate alone doesn't guarantee finite-sample false positive control in the sequential setting - that requires additional assumptions about the dependence structure. The text should be corrected to state: "We prove that this strategy results in an unbiased estimate of the model accuracy; as a consequence, the per-step false positive rate bound holds at each individual time step."

comments.title: Mischaracterization of sequential test applicability
comments.quote: However, as discussed above, they assume that the each distribution of the two samples does not change over time. In contrast, we are interested in the setting where the test examples might initially be from the same distribution as the training examples, but then shift at a later point in time. Sequential tests are not applicable to this setting.
comments.explanation: This claim mischaracterizes the capabilities of sequential hypothesis testing methods. The cited works (Balsubramani and Ramdas 2015; Lhéritier and Cazals 2018, 2019; Manole and Ramdas 2021) actually address change detection scenarios where distributions can shift over time. For example, Manole and Ramdas (2021) explicitly consider settings where the alternative hypothesis involves a change point occurring at an unknown time. The paper should either correct this claim or provide specific citations showing that sequential tests cannot handle delayed shifts.

comments.title: Undefined true labels in empirical accuracy formula
comments.quote: $\hat{\mu}_{w,t}=\frac{1}{2w}\sum_{i=t-w+1}^{t}\left(\mathbbm{1}\left(\hat{y}_{i}=y_{i}\right)+\mathbbm{1}\left(\hat{y}_{i}^{\prime}=y_{i}^{\prime}\right)\right).$
comments.explanation:
The formula uses undefined true labels $y_i$ and $y_i'$. In the source-target classification task, true labels are known by construction: source samples have label 0 and target samples have label 1. The correct empirical accuracy should compare predictions against these known labels.

Rewrite $\hat{\mu}_{w,t}=\frac{1}{2w}\sum_{i=t-w+1}^{t}\left(\mathbbm{1}\left(\hat{y}_{i}=y_{i}\right)+\mathbbm{1}\left(\hat{y}_{i}^{\prime}=y_{i}^{\prime}\right)\right)$ as $\hat{\mu}_{w,t}=\frac{1}{2w}\sum_{i=t-w+1}^{t}\left(\mathbbm{1}\left(\hat{y}_{i}=0\right)+\mathbbm{1}\left(\hat{y}_{i}^{\prime}=1\right)\right)$ because the true labels are fixed by construction in the source-target classification problem.

comments.title: FPR bound violation in GI-shift scenario
comments.quote: For FPR, all algorithms always satisfy the FPR bound (i.e.,  $\mathrm{FPR} \leq \alpha$ ).
comments.explanation: This claim is contradicted by the data in Table 2b. In the GI-shift scenario at time 200, the Ours algorithm shows FPR = 0.83%, which exceeds the α = 1% bound. Similarly, H5 shows FPR = 0.85% at time 200. The statement that 'all algorithms always satisfy the FPR bound' is therefore false. The authors should either correct this claim to acknowledge the violations or explain why these exceedances are acceptable within their framework.

comments.title: Undefined notation in probability statement
comments.quote: $\mathbbm{P}_{S_{w,t}\sim\mathcal{S}^{w},T_{w,t}\sim\mathcal{T}_{t-w+1:t}}\left[\hat{f}(S_{w,t},T_{w,t};\alpha)=0\right]\geq 1-\alpha.$
comments.explanation: The first-pass reviewer correctly notes that $\mathcal{T}_{t-w+1:t}$ is not defined as a single distribution. Earlier, $\mathcal{T}_{t_{1}:t_{2}}$ was defined as a sequence of distributions $(\mathcal{T}_{t_{1}},\mathcal{T}_{t_{1}+1},\ldots,\mathcal{T}_{t_{2}})$, not as a product measure. The probability statement incorrectly treats this sequence as a single distribution. Add the definition "$\mathcal{T}_{t-w+1:t} = \prod_{k=t-w+1}^{t}\mathcal{T}_{k}$ denotes the product measure over the window of distributions" before the probability statement.

comments.title: Missing window initialization condition
comments.quote: In fact, $2w\hat{\mu}_{w,t}$ is a Binomial random variable with Binomial$(2w,\mu_{w,t}^{*})$; thus, the accuracy $\mu_{w,t}^{*}$ can be estimated by the Clopper-Pearson (CP) interval $\Theta_{\text{CP}}(2w\hat{\mu}_{w,t},2w;\alpha)$
comments.explanation:
The binomial assumption and CP interval require $t \geq w$ to have $2w$ samples, but the algorithm doesn't specify behavior for $t < w$. For early time steps, the window contains fewer than $w$ samples, making the binomial parameter $2w$ incorrect.

Add "For $t < w$, we use all available samples up to time $t$, replacing $2w$ with $2t$ in the binomial distribution and CP interval calculation" after the CP interval definition to handle the initialization phase.

comments.title: Undefined "correctness" in theoretical claim
comments.quote: We prove that this optimization preserves the correctness—i.e., our algorithm achieves a desired bound on the false positive rate.
comments.explanation: The term "correctness" is undefined and then equated solely with false positive rate control. This is insufficient because statistical test correctness requires both type I error control (false positives) AND type II error control (false negatives/detection power). By defining correctness only in terms of false positive rate, the paper makes an incomplete theoretical claim. The abstract should state "preserves false positive rate control" rather than the broader undefined term "correctness".

comments.title: Missing batch size effect on Wald test
comments.quote: That is, we wait for  $B$  samples to be collected from the target distribution before checking for covariate shift and the updating the source-target classifier; then, we begin collecting the next batch.
comments.explanation: The batching mechanism creates a fundamental problem for the Wald test baseline. Wald's SPRT is designed for sequential observation where each sample can trigger an immediate decision. With batch size $B=10$, the Wald test can only make decisions after observing 10 samples, which changes its statistical properties entirely. The sequential nature is lost, and the false positive rate bound no longer holds as stated. The paper needs to clarify whether the Wald test is applied to each batch (treating the batch as 10 independent Bernoulli trials) or if it somehow maintains true sequential behavior. If applied per batch, the test statistic and decision thresholds must be adjusted for the grouped observation structure. Add a sentence explaining: "For the Wald test, we apply the SPRT to each batch of $B=10$ samples, treating the batch sum as a Binomial($B,p$) random variable with appropriately adjusted likelihood ratio thresholds."

comments.title: Inconsistent target distribution notation
comments.quote: $\mathcal{Q}_{w,t}(x,y)\!=\!\frac{1}{2}\cdot\mathcal{S}(x)\cdot\mathbbm{1}(y=0)+\frac{1}{2}\cdot\tilde{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}(x)\cdot\mathbbm{1}(y=1).$
comments.explanation:
The notation $\tilde{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}$ appears without definition and differs from $\mathcal{T}_{w,t}$ used in Section 3.1. This creates ambiguity about whether it represents a different distribution.

Replace $\tilde{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}$ with $\mathcal{T}_{w,t}$ to maintain consistency with the notation established in Section 3.1 and used throughout the paper.

comments.title: Undefined average distribution notation
comments.quote: More precisely, we want to determine whether $\mathcal{S}\neq\bar{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}$, where $\bar{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}=\sum_{k=t-w+1}^{t}\mathcal{T}_{k}/w$
comments.explanation: The notation $\bar{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}=\sum_{k=t-w+1}^{t}\mathcal{T}_{k}/w$ is not mathematically rigorous. The sum of probability distributions divided by $w$ needs clarification - this could mean either a mixture distribution or an average of density functions. Define $\bar{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}$ explicitly as the mixture distribution: "$\bar{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}$ is the mixture distribution with density $p_{\bar{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}}(x) = \frac{1}{w}\sum_{k=t-w+1}^{t} p_{\mathcal{T}_{k}}(x)$".

comments.title: Missing definition of uniform distribution over training set
comments.quote: $t_{2}$. On time step $t$, we consider samples $x_{t}\sim\mathcal{S}$ and $x_{t}^{\prime}\sim\mathcal{T}_{t}$; in practice, $\mathcal{S}$ can be taken to be the uniform distribution over the training set. We let
comments.explanation: The first-pass reviewer correctly identifies that the uniform distribution over the training set lacks a formal definition. For theoretical analysis, this should be explicitly defined. Add the sentence: "For a finite training set $\{x_{i}\}_{i=1}^{n}$, the uniform distribution is defined as $\mathcal{S} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\delta_{x_{i}}$ where $\delta_{x}$ is the Dirac measure at $x$." after the mention of uniform distribution.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 uses undefined notation
comments.quote: Detect covariate shift if $0.5 \notin \Theta_{\mathrm{CP}}(2w\hat{\mu}_{w,t}, 2w; \alpha)$ (▷) Calibrated covariate shift detection
comments.explanation: The notation $\hat{\mu}_{w,t}$ appears in Algorithm 1 step 5 without definition. While the text mentions that accuracy is the mean of Bernoulli random variables, the specific empirical estimator used in the algorithm is not defined. Add "$\hat{\mu}_{w,t}$ = empirical accuracy of $\hat{g}_t$ on the $2w$ predictions in current window" before step 5 in Algorithm 1.

comments.title: Missing justification for 2w factor in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: Detect covariate shift if $0.5 \notin \Theta_{\mathrm{CP}}(2w\hat{\mu}_{w,t}, 2w; \alpha)$ (▷) Calibrated covariate shift detection
comments.explanation: The factor $2w$ in the CP interval parameters is not explained in the algorithm. Since the algorithm processes $w$ examples from source $S$ and $w$ examples from target $T_{w,t}$ at each step, there are $2w$ total predictions. Add "(▷ where $2w$ is total predictions: $w$ from source $S$ and $w$ from target $T_{w,t}$)" after step 5 to clarify this connection.

comments.title: Undefined test function notation
comments.quote:
\[ \hat{f}(S_{w,t},T_{w,t})\approx\begin{cases}1&\text{if }\mathcal{S}\neq\bar{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}\\
0&\text{otherwise.}\end{cases} \]
comments.explanation: The test function $\hat{f}$ is introduced without defining its output space or the meaning of the approximation symbol. The function appears to output binary values (0 or 1), but the approximation symbol $\approx$ is ambiguous for a discrete output. Clarify whether $\hat{f}: \mathcal{X}^w \times \mathcal{X}^w \to \{0,1\}$ and replace $\approx$ with "=" since the test is deterministic given the samples and threshold $\alpha$.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation in CP interval definition
comments.quote: Then, the CP interval  $\Theta_{\mathrm{CP}}(s, n; \alpha) \subseteq [0, 1]$  is an interval around  $\hat{\mu}$  containing  $\mu^*$  with probability at least  $1 - \alpha$ , i.e.
comments.explanation: The CP interval is defined as a function of $s$ (the count) but described as "an interval around $\hat{\mu}$". Since $\hat{\mu} = s/n$ is derived from $s$, this creates notational inconsistency. Rewrite as "the CP interval $\Theta_{\mathrm{CP}}(s, n; \alpha)$ is an interval around the empirical mean $\hat{\mu} = s/n$" to clarify the relationship between the function parameters and the interval center.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation in Theorem 3
comments.quote:
If $\mathcal{S}=\tilde{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}$, then for any $\hat{g}_{t}$, we have

$\mathbb{P}_{(S_{w,t},T_{w,t})\sim\mathcal{S}^{w}\times\mathcal{T}_{t-w+1:t}}\left[\hat{f}(S_{w,t},T_{w,t};\alpha)=0\right]\geq 1-\alpha.$ (2)
comments.explanation: The theorem hypothesis states $\mathcal{S}=\tilde{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}$, but the probability measure uses $\mathcal{S}^{w}\times\mathcal{T}_{t-w+1:t}$. Under the hypothesis, $\mathcal{T}_{t-w+1:t}$ should equal $\mathcal{S}$, making the product measure $\mathcal{S}^{w}\times\mathcal{S}$. The current notation creates a contradiction between the hypothesis and the probability space. Rewrite the theorem as: "If $\mathcal{S}=\mathcal{T}_{w,t}$, then for any $\hat{g}_{t}$, we have $\mathbb{P}_{(S_{w,t},T_{w,t})\sim\mathcal{S}^{w}\times\mathcal{S}}\left[\hat{f}(S_{w,t},T_{w,t};\alpha)=0\right]\geq 1-\alpha$" to maintain consistency between the hypothesis and the probability measure.

comments.title: Undefined notation in Theorem 5
comments.quote: $\mathbb{P}\left[\hat{f}(S_{w,t},T_{w,t};\alpha)=1\right]\geq F\left(2w-\lfloor a(w,\alpha)+1\rfloor;2w,\frac{1}{2}-\epsilon\right)+F\Big{(}\lceil b(w,\alpha)-1\rceil;2w,1\Big{)}.$ (3)
comments.explanation: The second term uses $F(\lceil b(w,\alpha)-1\rceil;2w,1)$ with $p=1$. For a binomial CDF, $p=1$ creates a degenerate distribution where $F(s;n,1)=0$ for $s<n$ and $F(s;n,1)=1$ for $s\geq n$. This makes the term either 0 or 1 depending on the ceiling function, which doesn't align with the intended bound behavior. The parameter should be $\frac{1}{2}+\epsilon$ to maintain symmetry with the first term. Rewrite as: $F\left(2w-\lfloor a(w,\alpha)+1\rfloor;2w,\frac{1}{2}-\epsilon\right)+F\Big{(}\lceil b(w,\alpha)-1\rceil;2w,\frac{1}{2}+\epsilon\Big{)}$

comments.title: Incorrect condition derivation in Theorem 5
comments.quote: the condition on $w$ suggests that the bound is valid when $w\geq 201$ given $\alpha=0.01$
comments.explanation: The condition $w-1-\lfloor\sqrt{w\log(2/\alpha)}\rfloor\geq 0$ with $\alpha=0.01$ gives $\log(2/\alpha)=\log(200)\approx5.3$. Testing values: $w=20$ gives $20-1-\lfloor\sqrt{106}\rfloor=20-1-10=9\geq0$, $w=10$ gives $10-1-\lfloor\sqrt{53}\rfloor=10-1-7=2\geq0$, but $w=5$ gives $5-1-\lfloor\sqrt{26.5}\rfloor=5-1-5=-1<0$. The minimal $w$ is actually 10, not 201. Remove the specific claim about $w\geq201$ or provide the correct minimal $w$ value.

comments.title: Missing Wald test detection results
comments.quote: Table 3a shows the number of target samples required by each algorithm to detect the first covariate shift in the detection rate of at least  $80\%$ .
comments.explanation: Table 3a shows "-" entries for the Wald algorithm across all scenarios and perturbation types, indicating no detection results are reported. However, the text describes this as showing results for "each algorithm" and the Wald algorithm is included in the FPR table. This creates an inconsistency - if Wald can compute FPR, it should also be able to detect shifts. Either the Wald detection results should be reported, or the text should explain why Wald failed to detect shifts in these scenarios while still computing FPR values.

comments.title: Ambiguous shift probability interpretation
comments.quote: after observing  $25\%$  target samples (i.e.,  $250^{th}$  samples for natural shift experiments and  $2500^{th}$  samples for synthetic shift experiments), covariate shift is applied to all target samples (with probability 1) by adding random perturbations for synthetic shift and by drawing samples from a target distribution for natural shift;
comments.explanation: The description of shift application is confusing and potentially inconsistent. It says "covariate shift is applied to all target samples (with probability 1)" but then mentions two different mechanisms: "adding random perturbations for synthetic shift and by drawing samples from a target distribution for natural shift." The phrase "with probability 1" is redundant if shift is applied to ALL samples. More importantly, Table 1 shows probabilities less than 1 for gradual shifts, but the text doesn't explain how these probabilities relate to the sampling mechanisms. Clarify whether: (1) each sample independently has probability p of being shifted, or (2) exactly p proportion of samples in that window are shifted. Rewrite as: "covariate shift is applied to all target samples by either adding random perturbations (synthetic shift) or drawing from the target distribution (natural shift). For gradual shifts, each sample independently experiences covariate shift with probability p as specified in Table 1."

comments.title: Overstated false positive rate guarantee
comments.quote: We prove that even with this optimization, our approach provides finite sample guarantees on the false positive rate at a desired level
comments.explanation: This claim overstates the theoretical guarantee. Theorem 3 only provides a false positive rate bound for a single step t, not uniformly across all steps in the sequential setting. As acknowledged in Section 1, the false positive rate bound only holds per-step, not uniformly across all steps, which is a significant limitation for sequential detection applications.

comments.title: Mischaracterized false negative rate conditions
comments.quote: we also prove bounds on the false negative rate under a mild conditions on the trained classifier
comments.explanation: The conditions for the false negative rate bound in Theorem 5 are not mild. The bound requires w ≥ 201 for α = 0.01 and assumes the classifier achieves accuracy ≥ 1/2 + ε, which may be difficult to satisfy in high-dimensional settings with limited samples. These are restrictive practical limitations that should be acknowledged rather than described as mild conditions.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated sample efficiency claim
comments.quote: Our approach ensures sample efficiency by avoiding the need to split the dataset into a training set and a held-out test set, and instead using all the data to both train the source-target discriminator and to evaluate its accuracy
comments.explanation: This claim of sample efficiency is not theoretically substantiated. While the method uses all data for both training and testing, the theoretical analysis does not establish that this leads to better detection performance or requires fewer samples than alternative approaches. The empirical comparison is only against a held-out baseline, not against methods that might also use data efficiently.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes an online variant of the Classifier Two-Sample Test (C2ST) for detecting covariate shift in streaming data. By updating a binary discriminator online and evaluating its accuracy on incoming samples before performing gradient updates, the authors claim to maintain valid false positive rate control via Clopper-Pearson intervals without requiring a separate held-out test set.

The paper presents a clever and computationally efficient approach to sequential covariate shift detection that addresses common sample-efficiency bottlenecks. While the theoretical framework for validity is interesting, the reliance on the assumption that the classifier's accuracy remains a valid Bernoulli parameter in a non-stationary online setting requires more rigorous justification. The empirical results on ImageNet are compelling, though the scope of the shift types tested could be broader.

Overview issues:

- Independence of Sequential Predictions: Lemma 1 claims that predictions $\hat{y}_i$ are conditionally independent given the history of the classifier. However, in an online learning setting where the classifier $\hat{g}_t$ is updated at every step $t$ using the most recent samples, the classifier parameters are deterministic functions of the entire history of samples. This creates a dependency structure that likely violates the i.i.d. assumption required for the Clopper-Pearson interval to strictly hold as an exact confidence interval. The authors should clarify if the 'conditional independence' refers to the randomness of the labels or the model parameters, and provide a simulation or theoretical argument showing that the coverage probability of the CP interval does not degrade under the drift scenarios considered.

- Sensitivity to Classifier Architecture and Hyperparameters: The detection power of the proposed method is inherently tied to the expressivity and training dynamics of the source-target classifier $\hat{g}_t$. The paper assumes that an optimal or near-optimal classifier exists, but in a streaming setting, the model might suffer from catastrophic forgetting or slow adaptation if the learning rate is poorly tuned. There is no discussion on how the choice of architecture (e.g., the 128-unit MLP) or the learning rate interacts with the detection delay. A sensitivity analysis regarding the learning rate or model capacity would strengthen the claim that the detector is robust across different types of covariate shifts.

- Distinction Between Covariate and Concept Shift: The introduction correctly identifies the focus on covariate shift ($p(x)$ changes, $p(y|x)$ constant), but the detection mechanism relies on a binary classifier distinguishing source from target. If a concept shift occurs—where the relationship between features and labels changes—the discriminator will likely also learn to distinguish the distributions, triggering a false alarm for covariate shift. The paper lacks a discussion on how to interpret a positive detection result or how the method behaves when both covariate and concept shifts are present. Adding a synthetic experiment where $p(y|x)$ is intentionally perturbed would help characterize the detector's specificity.

- Theoretical Bounds for Non-Stationary Streams: Theorem 3 provides a false positive rate bound under the assumption $\mathcal{S} = \tilde{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}$. However, in a streaming context where the shift is gradual (as in the GI-shift scenario), the distribution $\tilde{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}$ is a mixture of distributions that may be changing at every time step. The theoretical derivation does not explicitly account for the bias introduced by the sliding window averaging over a non-stationary target distribution. It would improve the paper to explicitly state the conditions under which the CP interval remains valid when the target distribution is not constant within the window $w$.

- Lack of empirical validation for false positive rate under non-stationary streams: The paper relies on Theorem 3 to guarantee the false positive rate, but this theorem assumes the target distribution is stationary within the window w. In the gradual shift scenarios, the target distribution is changing at every time step, meaning the window average is a mixture of distinct distributions. The paper needs a simulation or a theoretical bound that evaluates the false positive rate when the target distribution is non-stationary within the sliding window, as this is the primary setting where the algorithm is intended to operate.

- Missing analysis of the detector specificity to covariate shift versus concept drift: The methodology uses a binary classifier to distinguish between source and target covariates, but it does not formally address the behavior when concept drift (p(y|x) changes) is present. Because the classifier is trained to distinguish source from target, it will likely flag concept drift as a covariate shift, leading to false positives regarding the nature of the shift. A controlled synthetic experiment—where p(y|x) is perturbed while keeping p(x) constant—is necessary to demonstrate whether the detector can distinguish between these two types of distribution changes.

- Absence of sensitivity analysis for the window size parameter: The detection performance and the validity of the Clopper-Pearson interval are highly dependent on the window size w. The paper uses w=10 throughout the experiments but does not provide a sensitivity analysis showing how varying w affects the trade-off between detection delay and the false positive rate. Adding a plot or table showing the detection delay and empirical FPR for different values of w, such as 5, 20, and 50, would provide the necessary guidance for practitioners to configure the algorithm for different streaming environments.

Recommendation: minor revision

Revision targets:

- Clarify the validity of the Clopper-Pearson interval in the online setting by addressing the dependency of the classifier $\hat{g}_t$ on previous samples.

- Include a sensitivity analysis of the detection delay with respect to the learning rate and model capacity of the source-target classifier.

- Add a discussion or experiment distinguishing the detector's response to pure covariate shift versus concept drift.

- Provide a more robust theoretical justification for the false positive rate bound when the target distribution is non-stationary within the sliding window.

comments.title: Invalid i.i.d. assumption for Clopper-Pearson interval
comments.quote: In fact, $2w\hat{\mu}_{w,t}$ is a Binomial random variable with Binomial$(2w,\mu_{w,t}^{*})$; thus, the accuracy $\mu_{w,t}^{*}$ can be estimated by the Clopper-Pearson (CP) interval
comments.explanation: The Clopper-Pearson interval assumes that the underlying random variables are i.i.d. Bernoulli trials. However, because the classifier $\hat{g}_t$ is updated at every step $t$ based on the history of samples, the indicators $\mathbb{1}(\hat{g}_i(x_i)=y_i)$ are neither identically distributed nor independent. The sum of these variables follows a Poisson binomial distribution rather than a Binomial distribution. Consequently, the standard Clopper-Pearson interval does not provide the claimed coverage guarantee. Please either provide a proof that the sequence of indicators is exchangeable or acknowledge that the interval is an approximation.

comments.title: Contradictory claims regarding sequential test usage
comments.quote: Sequential detection vs. sequential tests. While we consider the sequential setting, we deliberately choose not use a sequential hypothesis test, since the covariate shift may occur after a delay or gradually over time. A sequential test only applies if all of the test data is shifted.
comments.explanation: The claim that sequential hypothesis tests only apply if 'all of the test data is shifted' is incorrect. Methods such as the Sequential Probability Ratio Test (SPRT) or CUSUM are specifically designed to detect changes in distribution at unknown time points, including gradual or delayed shifts. By stating that sequential tests are inapplicable, the paper mischaracterizes the field. Please revise this justification to clarify that your choice of a per-step bound is a design preference rather than a limitation of sequential testing methodology.

comments.title: Look-ahead bias in online evaluation
comments.quote: Furthermore, it evaluates test accuracy using each new covariate before taking a gradient step; this strategy avoids constructing a held-out test set, which can reduce sample efficiency.
comments.explanation: Evaluating accuracy on the same sample used for a gradient update introduces look-ahead bias. Standard classifier-based two-sample tests rely on accuracy on a held-out set to ensure the test statistic is independent of the training process. Using the same sample for both evaluation and the gradient step means the classifier is effectively overfitting to the specific sample being evaluated, which invalidates the i.i.d. assumptions required for the Clopper-Pearson interval. Please clarify how the proof accounts for this bias or acknowledge that this is an approximation.

comments.title: Unjustified assumption of nontrivial accuracy in Theorem 5
comments.quote: Assume $\hat{g}_{t}$ achieves nontrivial accuracy, *i.e.*, $\mu_{w,t}^{*}\geq 1/2+\epsilon$, where $\epsilon\in(0,1/2]$, is the accuracy at distinguishing $\mathcal{S}$ and $\tilde{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}$.
comments.explanation: Theorem 5 assumes $\mu_{w,t}^* \geq 1/2 + \epsilon$ as a premise, but the justification relies on the VC dimension of the model family $\mathcal{G}$ to guarantee this. In an online setting where $\hat{g}_t$ is updated incrementally, convergence to the optimal classifier in $\mathcal{G}$ is not guaranteed without specific assumptions on the learning rate or loss function. Please add a condition specifying that the online learning algorithm is a consistent estimator of the optimal classifier to justify the use of the VC bound.

comments.title: Notation for false positive rate bound
comments.quote:
te. In particular, given $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}_{>0}$, if $\mathcal{S}=\bar{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}$, we want to ensure

$\mathbbm{P}_{S_{w,t}\sim\mathcal{S}^{w},T_{w,t}\sim\mathcal{T}_{t-w+1:t}}\left[\hat{f}(S_{w,t},T_{w,t};\alpha)=0\right]\geq 1-\alpha.$

Ideal
comments.explanation: While this expression is mathematically equivalent to the standard definition of a false positive rate, it is presented in a non-standard way. The test $\hat{f}$ is defined such that $\hat{f}=1$ indicates a rejection of the null hypothesis. A false positive occurs when the test rejects the null hypothesis even though it is true. To align with standard statistical practice, rewrite the inequality as $\mathbb{P}_{S_{w,t}\sim\mathcal{S}^{w},T_{w,t}\sim\mathcal{T}_{t-w+1:t}}[\hat{f}(S_{w,t},T_{w,t};\alpha)=1] \leq \alpha$.

comments.title: Ambiguity in target distribution sequence notation
comments.quote: We let $S_{w,t}=(x_{t-w+1},x_{t-w+2},\ldots,x_{t})$ and $T_{w,t}=(x_{t-w+1}^{\prime},x_{t-w+2}^{\prime},\ldots,x_{t}^{\prime})$ denote the recent examples in a time window of a given size $w\in\mathbb{N}$.
comments.explanation: The definition of $T_{w,t}$ as a sequence of samples is clear, but the null hypothesis $H_0: \mathcal{S} = \bar{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}$ uses the average distribution. It is not explicitly stated whether the samples $x_k^{\prime}$ in $T_{w,t}$ are drawn from the individual distributions $\mathcal{T}_k$ or from the mixture distribution $\bar{\mathcal{T}}_{w,t}$. Please clarify by adding a sentence specifying that each $x_k^{\prime}$ is drawn independently from $\mathcal{T}_k$ for $k \in \{t-w+1, \dots, t\}$.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
